content.text: What the Incident Commander Actually Does
The Incident Commander (IC) is the single person responsible for the outcome of an incident, but they are
not

responsible for fixing it. The distinction matters: engineers on the call diagnose, investigate, and implement mitigations. The IC tracks the passage of time, drives decisions when the team is stuck or divided, manages communication to stakeholders, and calls the incident resolved or escalates it when the response stalls. An IC who starts debugging code has stopped being an IC — they have become a responder without anyone owning the coordination role.

This separation is especially important for GenAI incidents, where the investigation often spans multiple system boundaries simultaneously — LLM provider status, application code, retrieval pipelines, prompt configurations, and cost metrics. Without a dedicated coordinator, the team fragments into parallel investigations with no one synthesizing the findings or making the call to activate a fallback versus wait for a provider fix.

attrs.alt: Incident Commander role diagram: IC at center coordinating on-call engineers (diagnose/mitigate), communications lead (status page/Slack), and stakeholders/legal. Roster shows primary, shadow, and backup IC roles with eligibility criteria. Timeline shows IC actions from T+0 to resolution.

content.text: IC Responsibilities in the First 60 Minutes

The IC's action timeline is strict. GenAI incidents move fast in both directions — a provider outage can recover in 10 minutes or persist for 4 hours — and the IC must make several structured decisions at fixed time checkpoints rather than waiting for certainty.
T+0 to T+5: Acknowledge and Establish Command
Acknowledge the alert in the paging system within 5 minutes. Unacknowledged alerts escalate automatically to the backup IC.
Open the incident war room: a dedicated Slack channel named
#inc-YYYYMMDD-NNN
and a video bridge. The channel is the single source of truth for the incident timeline.
Post the opening status message (template below) so responders and observers have immediate context.
Page the on-call engineer for the affected system. If the alert already paged them, confirm they are engaged.
Designate a Communications Lead if the incident is SEV 1 or SEV 2. For SEV 3, the IC handles comms directly.

# Incident War Room Opening Message (post immediately to #inc-YYYYMMDD-NNN)

**IC:** @alice
**Status:** INVESTIGATING
**Declared:** 2026-07-02 09:14 UTC
**Alert:** GenAIAvailabilitySLOBreach — LLM error rate 23% (threshold 5%)
**Scope:** Subset of users (gpt-4o endpoint only; claude-* unaffected)
**Severity:** SEV 2 (default) — scope assessment in progress
**Runbook:** https://runbooks.internal/genai/availability

**Current situation:**
- LLM error rate spiked to 23% at 09:12 UTC
- Provider status page: all clear (as of 09:15 UTC)
- No deploy in the past 2 hours
- Responder: @bob is investigating

**Next update:** 09:29 UTC (15 min)
T+5 to T+15: Confirm Classification and Scope

By T+15 the IC must have confirmed: (1) the incident category from the decision tree, (2) the scope (single user, subset, or all-users), and therefore (3) the final severity level. This is the window for scope assessment, not deep diagnosis. If scope is unclear, default to the broader scope (higher severity) and downgrade once confirmed.
Ask the responder for a one-sentence status update every 5 minutes: 'What are you looking at right now? What have you ruled out?'
Post the first status update to the Slack channel at T+15 regardless of whether there is new information
For SEV 1: notify the on-call manager and legal (if safety/compliance) at T+15 if not already done
For SEV 1 with no mitigation plan by T+15: page the backup IC now — two ICs for complex SEV 1s is not overhead, it is risk management
T+15 to T+30: Drive Mitigation Decisions

The IC's primary job in this window is to prevent analysis paralysis. Engineers can stay in investigation mode indefinitely; the IC's job is to force a mitigation decision when enough information exists to act, even if the root cause is not fully understood. The decision framework:
Is there an available mitigation that stops user impact, even if it is not the root-cause fix? (Fallback model, circuit break, feature disable) → deploy it now, continue diagnosis on the side
Is the root cause clearly internal (a recent deploy, a config change)? → rollback immediately rather than continuing to diagnose
Is the root cause clearly external (provider outage confirmed on status page)? → activate fallback model, open provider support ticket, set a 30-minute checkpoint for provider recovery
None of the above? → escalate to a specialist (ML engineer, provider TAM) and set a 15-minute checkpoint

IC rule: 'Good enough to stop the bleeding' beats 'waiting for the perfect diagnosis'. A feature that is disabled for 2 hours costs less than a feature that is serving bad output for 2 hours while the team debates root cause.
T+30 to Resolution: Status Cadence and Handoff

Post a status update every 30 minutes to the incident channel. The update format is: current status, what is known, what is being done, when the next update will be. Do not post 'still investigating' with no additional information — if you have nothing new to report, the update should explain what specifically you are waiting for and why.

# 30-Minute Status Update Template

**Status:** MITIGATING  (was: INVESTIGATING)
**Time:** 09:44 UTC  (T+30)
**IC:** @alice

**What happened:** OpenAI gpt-4o endpoint returning 503s. Provider status page
updated at 09:38 UTC confirming partial outage in us-east-1.

**What we did:** Activated fallback to claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 at 09:32 UTC.
Error rate dropped from 23% to 0.8% (residual from in-flight requests).

**Current user impact:** Minimal. Users are being served by fallback model.
Response quality may differ from primary model for complex queries.

**What we are waiting for:** Provider resolution. Last ETA from status page: 10:30 UTC.

**Next update:** 10:14 UTC (30 min) or sooner if provider resolves.

If the incident extends beyond 2 hours, the IC must evaluate whether to hand off to a fresh IC. The IC on a long-running incident accumulates context bias — they become invested in a particular hypothesis — and fatigue degrades decision quality. A structured handoff to a fresh IC resets both.
Roster Structure and Rotation

The IC roster is the list of engineers who are eligible and trained to serve as IC, organized into a weekly rotation. A roster that is too small burns out the same engineers repeatedly. A roster that is too large produces ICs who rotate in rarely enough to lose practice. The target size depends on team size, but a sustainable roster requires at least 6 engineers — enough for a 6-week rotation where each engineer is primary IC once every 6 weeks.
Role Definitions
Role
Responsibilities
Paging Order
Minimum Tenure
Primary IC
Owns the incident from declaration to resolution. Single point of coordination. Accountable for communication SLAs.
First page
Tier 2 / L5 + IC certification
Shadow IC
Observes all incidents during their shadow week. Takes handoff on request. Provides second opinion to primary.
Not paged (observer)
IC training module complete
Backup IC
Paged if primary does not acknowledge within 5 minutes. Can decline if already engaged in another active incident.
Second page (T+5)
IC certified · not currently on another active call
Incident Manager
For SEV 1 only: senior manager who owns stakeholder communication and executive escalation. IC hands off comms track only.
SEV 1 only · manual page by IC
Manager level · crisis comms training
Eligibility Criteria
Not every engineer should be on the IC roster on their first day. The following criteria ensure ICs have the context to make good decisions under pressure:
Minimum seniority: Tier 2 / L5 or equivalent. The IC must be able to make technical judgment calls and have organizational credibility to override engineers who are headed in the wrong direction.
IC training module: a written module covering the IC role, this roster structure, the communication SLAs, the escalation paths, and the legal notification requirements for safety incidents.

Three shadow incidents: the engineer must have observed at least three incidents as a shadow IC before taking a solo primary IC shift. This ensures they have seen the war room process in practice, not just in documentation.
GameDay simulation: participation in at least one GameDay (a structured incident simulation) where the engineer practiced the IC role under conditions designed to resemble a real incident.

# IC Roster configuration (stored in version control)
# File: oncall/ic-roster.yaml

rotation:
  cadence: weekly
  handoff_day: Monday
  handoff_time: "09:00"
  timezone: "America/Los_Angeles"

eligible_ics:
  - id: alice
    name: Alice Chen
    tier: 2
    certified: true
    shadow_incidents_completed: 5
    gameday_completed: true
    currently_eligible: true

  - id: bob
    name: Bob Kumar
    tier: 2
    certified: true
    shadow_incidents_completed: 3
    gameday_completed: true
    currently_eligible: true

  - id: carol
    name: Carol Osei
    tier: 3  # staff
    certified: true
    shadow_incidents_completed: 8
    gameday_completed: true
    currently_eligible: true

  - id: dave
    name: Dave Matos
    tier: 2
    certified: false         # in progress
    shadow_incidents_completed: 1
    gameday_completed: false
    currently_eligible: false   # completes training next month

schedule:
  - week: "2026-W27"
    primary: alice
    shadow:  dave        # dave's third shadow week
    backup:  carol

  - week: "2026-W28"
    primary: bob
    shadow:  null
    backup:  alice

  - week: "2026-W29"
    primary: carol
    shadow:  null
    backup:  bob
Roster Automation with PagerDuty

Manual roster management (spreadsheet, calendar) breaks within months as engineers join, leave, or take leave. Automate the roster using the on-call scheduling tool's API to generate the weekly rotation from the
ic-roster.yaml
file and validate that each week has a certified primary, a certified backup, and that no engineer is scheduled as both primary IC and engineering on-call on the same week.

import yaml, datetime, itertools
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Iterator

@dataclass
class IcSlot:
    week: str          # ISO week: "2026-W27"
    primary: str
    shadow: str | None
    backup: str

def load_eligible(roster_path: str) -> list[dict]:
    with open(roster_path) as f:
        roster = yaml.safe_load(f)
    return [e for e in roster["eligible_ics"] if e["currently_eligible"]]

def generate_rotation(roster_path: str, start_week: str, n_weeks: int = 12) -> list[IcSlot]:
    eligible = load_eligible(roster_path)
    ids = [e["id"] for e in eligible]
    # Round-robin for primary
    primary_cycle: Iterator[str] = itertools.cycle(ids)
    # Shadow candidates: not yet certified or < 3 shadows
    all_ics = yaml.safe_load(open(roster_path))["eligible_ics"]
    shadows = [e["id"] for e in all_ics if not e["currently_eligible"] or e["shadow_incidents_completed"] < 3]

    slots = []
    base = datetime.datetime.strptime(start_week + "-1", "%G-W%V-%u")
    for week_offset in range(n_weeks):
        primary = next(primary_cycle)
        # Backup is the next eligible after primary
        backup = ids[(ids.index(primary) + 1) % len(ids)]
        shadow = shadows[week_offset % len(shadows)] if shadows else None
        week_str = (base + datetime.timedelta(weeks=week_offset)).strftime("%G-W%V")
        slots.append(IcSlot(week=week_str, primary=primary, shadow=shadow, backup=backup))
    return slots

def validate_slot(slot: IcSlot) -> list[str]:
    errors = []
    if slot.primary == slot.backup:
        errors.append(f"Week {slot.week}: primary and backup are the same person ({slot.primary})")
    if slot.shadow == slot.primary:
        errors.append(f"Week {slot.week}: shadow and primary are the same person")
    return errors

# Example usage
slots = generate_rotation("oncall/ic-roster.yaml", start_week="2026-W27", n_weeks=6)
for slot in slots:
    errs = validate_slot(slot)
    if errs:
        print("VALIDATION ERROR:", errs)
    else:
        print(f"{slot.week}: primary={slot.primary}, backup={slot.backup}, shadow={slot.shadow or '-'}")
Communication SLAs the IC Must Meet

The IC is accountable for meeting these communication SLAs during an active incident. Missing a communication SLA is itself a process failure that should appear in the postmortem, separate from the technical root cause.
Communication
SEV 1
SEV 2
SEV 3
War room open + opening status posted
T+5 min
T+10 min
T+15 min
On-call manager notified
T+5 min
T+15 min
T+30 min
Legal notified (safety/compliance only)
T+30 min
T+30 min
T+30 min
First status page update
T+10 min
T+20 min
T+30 min
Recurring status update cadence
Every 15 min
Every 30 min
Every 60 min
Backup IC paged if no mitigation plan
T+15 min
T+30 min
Not automatic
Executive stakeholder loop
T+30 min
T+60 min
Not required
GenAI-Specific: Legal Notification for Safety Incidents

The 30-minute legal notification SLA for safety/compliance incidents is non-negotiable and must be met even if the incident is not yet fully characterized. The notification does not need to include root cause analysis — it needs to include: what happened (at the level of 'harmful output reached users' or 'potential PII leak detected'), the scope estimate, and the current mitigation status. Legal will tell the IC whether formal regulatory notification is required.

# Safety incident legal notification template
LEGAL_NOTIFICATION_TEMPLATE = """
Subject: [SAFETY INCIDENT] {incident_id} - {title}

This is a mandatory 30-minute safety incident notification.

Incident: {incident_id}
Declared: {declared_at} UTC
IC: {ic_name}
Category: Safety / Compliance

WHAT HAPPENED:
{description}

SCOPE ESTIMATE:
{scope}

CURRENT MITIGATION:
{mitigation_status}

ROOT CAUSE:
Unknown / under investigation

REGULATORY IMPLICATION (IC assessment):
{regulatory_assessment}
(e.g. 'Potential GDPR Art. 33 notification required - awaiting legal assessment')

Next update from IC: {next_update_time} UTC or sooner if situation changes.

This notification does not waive attorney-client privilege.
Please do not forward outside legal counsel without IC approval.
""".strip()

def send_legal_notification(incident: dict) -> None:
    '''Call this by T+30 for any safety/compliance incident.'''
    body = LEGAL_NOTIFICATION_TEMPLATE.format(**incident)
    # Send via your internal notification system
    print(body)  # replace with actual send
Shadow IC Program: Building the Roster

An IC roster that only grows by assigning unsuspecting engineers to a live incident is a roster that produces bad ICs. The shadow program is a structured apprenticeship that gives candidates a safe path to certification.
Complete the IC training module (written, self-paced, ~2 hours). Covers: role boundaries, communication SLAs, escalation paths, legal notification requirements.
Shadow 3 incidents as a designated shadow IC. The shadow IC is named in the incident record, observes the war room, takes notes on IC decisions, and debrief with the primary IC post-incident.

Complete one GameDay simulation in the IC role. A GameDay is a scheduled, controlled incident simulation where the team runs through a prepared scenario. The shadow practices the war room opening, status updates, and a handoff.
Sign-off from a certified IC (typically the IC from the third shadow incident or the GameDay facilitator).
Track shadow progress in the
ic-roster.yaml
file so the rotation generator can identify who needs shadow slots and automatically pair them with a primary IC who has capacity to mentor.
Summary

The Incident Commander role is a coordination function, not a technical function. The IC acknowledges alerts, opens the war room, confirms classification and scope, drives mitigation decisions at fixed checkpoints, meets communication SLAs, and hands off when the incident extends. The roster requires at least 6 certified ICs in a weekly rotation, with shadow and backup roles defined for each week. Certification requires: IC training module, three shadow incidents, and a GameDay simulation. For GenAI incidents, the most critical IC-specific obligation is the 30-minute legal notification for safety and compliance incidents — which must be met regardless of whether the root cause is understood.
